senses.definition: to do something that is very likely to result in trouble or difficulty

examples.x: Driving without a seatbelt is like asking for trouble; it's dangerous and against the law.
Borrowing money from a loan shark is definitely asking for trouble due to the exorbitant interest rates.
Joining that contentious online debate and expressing strong opinions is a sure way to ask for trouble and confrontation with other users.

senses.definition: a person that one is opposed to and fights or competes with

examples.x: In the final round of the chess tournament, he faced his toughest adversary yet.
Even though they were fierce adversaries on the court, off the court they were good friends.
As a lawyer, she was used to meeting her adversary in the courtroom.
The chess player studied his adversary’s strategies before the tournament.
The general planned his tactics carefully to counter the enemy's adversary.

senses.definition: in opposition to someone or something

examples.x: She has been fighting against discrimination for years.
The team is playing against the national champions next week.
This medicine is effective against flu symptoms.
We must protect the environment against pollution.

senses.definition: used when a situation suddenly becomes very intense or chaotic

examples.x: When the referee made a controversial call, all hell broke loose on the soccer field as players from both teams engaged in a brawl.
As soon as the power went out during the storm, all hell broke loose in the dark, with people panicking and searching for flashlights.

senses.definition: a noisy dispute

examples.x: The altercation in the restaurant drew the attention of every diner.
A heated altercation occurred during the town hall meeting.
The witness described the altercation as intense and chaotic.
The altercation at the protest led to several arrests by the police.
The manager intervened to break up the altercation among the employees.

senses.definition: used to convey that one is against something
to speak to someone often angrily because one disagrees with them
a discussion, typically a serious one, between two or more people with different views

examples.x: His strong argument convinced the team to change their strategy.
She avoids getting into an argument with her boss.
The siblings had a silly argument about who got the bigger slice of cake.
Their constant arguments are causing problems in the relationship.
They had an argument about where to go for vacation.

senses.definition: to deny a statement

examples.x: When he claimed that the project was behind schedule, I had to argue with him, providing evidence that we were actually ahead of our timeline.
She didn't hesitate to argue with her colleague when he suggested that their team had failed to meet the client's expectations.
I found it difficult to argue with the assertion that our sales had dropped, as I had the data to support my counterclaim.
During the meeting, I had to argue with the notion that our marketing strategy was ineffective, highlighting the positive feedback we had received.
He often argues with the idea that hard work alone guarantees success, emphasizing the importance of opportunity and timing.

senses.definition: (of a person) ready to argue and often arguing

examples.x: His argumentative nature often leads to heated discussions with his peers.
The couple's argumentative relationship made family gatherings tense and uncomfortable.
Her argumentative demeanor made it difficult for others to work with her collaboratively.
The argumentative tone of the meeting hindered productive discussion and problem-solving.
Despite his argumentative tendencies, he was respected for his critical thinking skills.

senses.definition: used to introduce a statement that presents a truth or reality, often to clarify or emphasize something

examples.x: As a matter of fact, I did see him at the store earlier today.
He claimed it was the largest in the city, but as a matter of fact, there are several larger buildings in the downtown area.
You mentioned she wasn't interested; as a matter of fact, she expressed keen interest in joining the project
You may believe it's a rumor, but as a matter of fact, the company has officially announced the merger

senses.definition: opposite to; against
in serious disagreement with someone

examples.x: The negotiation between the union and management was at loggerheads over issues of worker compensation.
Despite being close friends, Jane and Susan were at loggerheads when it came to deciding on a vacation destination.
The political parties were at loggerheads, leading to a government shutdown due to the inability to pass a budget.
The siblings found themselves at loggerheads over the division of their late father's estate.
The team members were at loggerheads regarding the project approach, causing delays and inefficiencies in the workflow.

senses.definition: (of opinions) in complete disagreement with each other

examples.x: Despite being close friends, they were at odds over the best approach to solving the problem.
The team members were at odds regarding the strategy for the upcoming project, leading to a heated discussion.
The siblings found themselves at odds when it came to deciding how to spend their vacation.
The scientists were at odds about the interpretation of the experimental results, sparking a debate in the research community.
The committee members were at odds over the proposed changes to the policy, highlighting differing perspectives on the matter.

senses.definition: used when two or more people, groups, or organizations fight or disagree with one another

examples.x: The siblings were constantly at each other's throats, arguing over trivial matters.
During the heated debate, the political candidates were at each other's throats, exchanging harsh words and accusations.
The two business partners found themselves at each other's throats when their visions for the company's future diverged.
In the competitive world of sports, rival teams are often at each other's throats, vying for victory and dominance.
The neighbors were at each other's throats over a property dispute, leading to a protracted legal battle.

senses.definition: feeling of anger between people, particularly because of an argument
to forcefully make people stop arguing and start behaving appropriately
a situation in which opposing sides argue or compete with one another to achieve something
the dividing line between opposing sides in a conflict or confrontation

examples.x: The battle line in the debate was drawn over the issue of gun control.
In the heated debate over healthcare reform, it was clear that battle lines were drawn between those advocating for a universal system and those supporting a private-market approach.
In the political arena, battle lines are sharply defined between liberals and conservatives on issues such as gun control and taxation.
The battle lines were drawn over the proposed budget cuts.

senses.definition: a subject of dispute; a situation in which people disagree

word_list.pronunciation: bˈɪɹd ðə lˈaɪən ɪn hɪz hɜː dˈɛn

senses.definition: to confront someone very powerful or dangerous in their area of control and strength, where they hold the advantage

examples.x: She decides to beard the lion in his den by confronting the powerful CEO about unethical business practices.
They are bearding the lion in his den by challenging the oppressive government in peaceful protests.
The investigative journalist plans to beard the lion in his den by exposing the corrupt politician's misdeeds.
He bearded the lion in his den when he confronted the notorious gang leader in his own territory.
The activists bearded the lion in her den by organizing a protest outside the corporate headquarters.

senses.definition: displaying a willingness to start an argument, fight, or war

examples.x: The politician's bellicose speeches fueled animosity among the constituents, polarizing the community.
Jake's bellicose attitude often leads to arguments with his classmates.

senses.definition: the desire to start an argument, fight, or war

word_list.pronunciation: biː nˌɑːt hˈævɪŋ ˌɛni ʌv ɪt ʌv ðæt

senses.definition: to be completely unwilling to do something
to argue over unimportant things in an ongoing and repetitive way

examples.x: The siblings continued to bicker over who got to use the TV remote, each insisting on their preferred channel.
Despite their strong friendship, the friends would occasionally bicker about insignificant matters, like choosing a movie.
The couple tended to bicker over household chores, leading to frequent and minor disagreements.
Team members started to bicker during the project, arguing about small details and slowing down progress.
Neighbors would often bicker about parking spaces, causing tension in the community.

senses.definition: argument over unimportant things
to not say something, against one's wish, in order to avoid causing an argument or upsetting someone

examples.x: She often has to bite her tongue during meetings to avoid getting into arguments.
I bite my tongue when my parents give me unsolicited advice to maintain harmony in the family.
He regretted not biting his tongue during the heated argument; his harsh words caused irreparable damage.
Despite her strong disagreement, she bit her tongue to keep the peace during the negotiation.
In sensitive situations, it's important to know when to bite your tongue and choose diplomacy over confrontation.
If you want to avoid unnecessary conflict, sometimes it's better to bite your tongue and let the conversation move on.

senses.definition: a subject over which people disagree

examples.x: The allocation of funds became the bone of contention during the budget meeting, leading to heated debates among department heads.
In the family discussion about holiday plans, the choice of destination became a bone of contention, with each member advocating for a different location.
The issue of resource distribution has been a long-standing bone of contention between the two neighboring countries.
The decision to implement new policies became the bone of contention between the management and the employees, sparking protests in the workplace.
When negotiating the contract, the compensation package emerged as the primary bone of contention, delaying the agreement between the employer and the candidate.

senses.definition: said to introduce a statement that displays one's surprise, anger, or disagreement
to continue talking

examples.x: She wouldn't stop carrying on about her new job all day.
He carried on about the weather even though no one was listening.
They carried on about the movie for hours after it ended.
Stop carrying on about your problems and try to enjoy the party!
He continued to carry on about his latest project, despite others losing interest.

senses.definition: intending to provoke thought or discussion

examples.x: His challenging questions made everyone rethink their assumptions.
The film presented a challenging perspective on modern society.
She offered a challenging opinion that no one expected.

senses.definition: a serious argument between two sides caused by their different views and beliefs

examples.x: The clash between the two political parties escalated into a heated debate over healthcare reform.
The cultural festival was disrupted by a clash of opinions on how the event should be organized.
The courtroom was tense as the lawyers engaged in a clash over the interpretation of the evidence.
The clash between environmental activists and industrial developers resulted in a series of protests.
The board meeting ended abruptly due to a clash among the members about the future direction of the company.

senses.definition: to strongly and publicly argue or disagree with someone

examples.x: The two politicians clashed during the debate over healthcare policies.
They clashed over how to divide the responsibilities for the project.
The manager clashed with his employees about the new company rules.
Activists clashed with authorities over environmental regulations.
The siblings often clashed about who should take care of their parents.

senses.definition: (of people, their opinions, ideas, etc.) to seriously disagree

examples.x: Their ideas about how to handle the project collided, causing a delay in progress.
The two leaders collided over the terms of the peace agreement.
The group of friends collided over where to go for their vacation.
Their views on politics collided, making it hard to have a civil conversation.
The parents collided on how to discipline their child, causing tension at home.

senses.definition: a serious disagreement between people, ideas, opinions, etc.
eager and ready to start an argument or fight
used to tell someone that they should stop saying or doing a particular thing

examples.x: Come off it now – she was only trying to help.
Come off it, you can't expect us to believe that you caught a fish the size of a car.
She said she won the race by a mile, and her friends replied, "Come off it; it was a close finish."
He told us he could make us millionaires overnight; we had to tell him to come off it.

senses.definition: to win an argument or other competitive situation

examples.x: The athlete trained hard and was able to come out on top in the competition, winning the gold medal.

senses.definition: a serious disagreement or argument, often involving opposing interests or ideas

examples.x: The conflict between the two departments over resource allocation was causing delays in the project.
Their long-standing conflict was finally resolved through open communication and compromise.
The conflict between the two countries escalated into a full-scale war.
She tried to mediate the conflict between her friends, aiming to restore their relationship.

senses.definition: (of two ideas, opinions, etc.) to oppose each other

examples.x: His personal beliefs often conflict with the principles of his profession, leading to internal struggles.
Can you please explain why your statement conflicts with the information provided in the textbook?
His actions often conflict with his stated intentions.

senses.definition: to face someone, particularly in a way that is unfriendly or threatening

examples.x: The manager confronted the employee about the missing inventory.
She summoned the courage to confront her abusive partner about his behavior.
The principal had to confront the students who were bullying their classmates.
The neighbors confronted the noisy partygoers about the disturbance late at night.
She confronted her friend about spreading rumors behind her back.

senses.definition: a situation of hostility or strong disagreement between two opposing individuals, parties, or groups

examples.x: The heated confrontation between the neighbors stemmed from a dispute over property boundaries.
The diplomatic efforts aimed to prevent a military confrontation between the two nations.
The police were called to intervene in a confrontation between protesters and counter-protesters.
The teacher mediated a confrontation between two students who had been arguing during recess.
The heated confrontation in the courtroom arose from conflicting testimonies of the witnesses.

senses.definition: likely to cause arguments because of being aggressive
a state of heated disagreement, often coming from different viewpoints or interests

examples.x: The new policy caused contention among the board members.
Their contention over the budget led to several heated meetings.
The negotiation process was marked by contention over contract terms.
The historical account was a source of contention among scholars.

senses.definition: inclined to argue or provoke disagreement

examples.x: The contentious individual frequently initiated arguments and disagreements within the group.
Her contentious nature made it challenging to reach consensus in team discussions.
The family gathering became tense as the contentious relative expressed dissenting opinions.
Despite efforts to maintain a harmonious atmosphere, the contentious coworker stirred up controversy in the office.
As a contentious debater, he enjoyed challenging opposing viewpoints in intellectual discussions.

senses.definition: to disagree with someone, particularly by asserting the opposite of their statement

examples.x: She contradicted his claim that the project was on schedule.
He contradicted the teacher’s explanation, arguing that it was inaccurate.
The witness contradicted the defendant's testimony during the trial.
They contradicted each other during the debate, with no clear agreement.
She contradicted him by providing a different perspective on the issue.

senses.definition: someone who acts against popular opinion, particularly in investment markets
a slight disagreement or an unpleasant event that causes embarrassment

examples.x: Their evening was interrupted by a minor contretemps over the choice of restaurant.
The unexpected contretemps at the meeting left everyone feeling a bit awkward.
She tried to brush off the contretemps with her colleague, but it still left her flustered.
The contretemps during the conference call was quickly resolved, but it left a lasting impression.
The guest speaker handled the contretemps with grace, turning the embarrassing moment into a joke.

senses.definition: causing a lot of strong public disagreement or discussion

examples.x: The politician's controversial statements about immigration sparked heated discussions among voters.
The documentary was controversial, sparking debates across the country.
The controversial film explored sensitive social issues, prompting both praise and criticism from viewers.
Many people find her statements on the issue controversial.
The new movie has been criticized for its controversial themes.
Some of the controversial ideas in the article were challenged by experts.
She made a controversial claim about the health benefits of the diet.

senses.definition: in a way that causes strong public disagreement

examples.x: The decision to cut funding for the public library was controversially debated in the community.
The book explored a controversially provocative topic, generating conflicting opinions among readers.
The artist's latest work was exhibited controversially, eliciting diverse reactions from the audience.
The new policy was implemented controversially, sparking heated discussions among employees.
The politician's statement on the hot-button issue was controversially received, dividing public opinion.

senses.definition: a strong disagreement or argument over something that involves many people

examples.x: The new policy sparked a controversy among community members.
She wrote an article about the ongoing controversy in the education system.
The film's portrayal of historical events caused significant controversy.
His comments during the interview led to a media controversy.
The controversy over the environmental impact of the project was widely discussed.
